artifacts.content:
Hi Daniel,

Kids are finally in bed, so I’m catching my breath and trying to get my brain around everything before tomorrow. I was up way too late last night running numbers again. Between Evan’s new soccer cleats, Harper’s art club fees, and gas for all the Plano trips to help Mom after her knee surgery, I feel like we’re constantly playing financial Tetris.

I keep thinking about how last school year I ended up spending around $1,200 of our own money on the Lincoln STEM lab – little Arduino kits, LEDs, filament, copper tape, all the stuff that makes the mobile makerspace work. I still can’t believe $600 of that went on the 0% interest card right after your overtime at the healthcare IT company in Irving got cut in January 2024. I know we agreed it was temporary, but it nags at me every time I open the statement.

Part of why I’m pushing so hard to get these carts fully stocked is so we’re not quietly subsidizing the whole thing out of our family budget again. The kids at Lincoln, especially my Title I group, really do light up when they get to build instead of just read, but I also don’t want us one surprise car repair away from panic.

On the bright side, Evan’s been more excited about school since he started hanging out in the STEM lab after class, and Harper keeps begging me to bring home “scrap cardboard treasures” for her. So at least our chaos is producing some joy.

Can we sit down this weekend and look at that 0% card together and make a plan to pay off the makerspace chunk before the promo period ends?

Love,
Melissa

Hi Mom,

I wanted to catch you up before the week gets away from me again. Things at Lincoln are their usual circus. I’m still the unofficial “STEM person,” rolling that poor battered cart up and down the 7th grade hallway every day.

The kids are working on community problem-solving projects, and a lot of it actually grew out of what happened with Evan’s leg last October. When he broke it and had to use the wheelchair for six weeks, getting him around Lincoln was such a nightmare. Remember those frustrating meetings in the front office with Assistant Principal Karen Delgado about the lack of ramps near the 7th-grade science wing? I left one of them in tears in October 2023 because we couldn’t even get him to my room 214 without backtracking forever.

After that, some of my students started staying after school in 214 to tinker with little accessibility gadgets – simple door openers, buzzer ideas for ramp areas, etc. It was all very cobbled together with cardboard and hot glue, but they were so into it. Now I’m trying to turn that energy into something more organized, with a mobile makerspace that can travel to different classrooms and focus on real accessibility issues on campus.

Evan’s doing better now, and he actually likes telling the younger kids what worked and didn’t when he was rolling around in that chair. Harper, meanwhile, just wants to decorate every prototype with glitter.

How are you feeling after your last follow-up? Text me your appointment dates again so I can put them in my calendar.

Love you,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Evan, ramps, and my latest STEM chaos

artifacts.content:
Brain dump – makerspace rollout thoughts

I keep circling the same questions about who this mobile STEM makerspace is really for. On paper it’s “6–8th grade, ~90 kids,” but in reality I know exactly who will end up in the room. Right now I probably only have 8–10 students who are genuinely nerdy-excited about circuits and design thinking. The rest will be the ones who got parked in my advisory or science sections because they needed somewhere to go and no one else wanted the behavior headaches.

That’s not necessarily bad; a lot of my “frequent flyers” in the office actually do better with their hands busy. Most of the kids I’m targeting are the ones who struggle to stay on task, drift during notes, and rack up minor write-ups. If I can give them a structured project to own during the day, maybe I can keep them out of ISS and give them something to be proud of.

I also need to be honest with myself: because Evan is a 6th grader at Lincoln now, I’m absolutely going to pilot a lot of this with his class first. I know his group, I know their quirks, and it feels safer to test the logistics with kids who already know my routines before I start pushing the cart into other teams’ rooms.

To do: sketch a simple rotation schedule; talk to Ms. Ramirez about which students with IEPs/ELL needs would benefit most; build in roles for quieter kids who hate presenting.

Hey Kayla,

I owe you about twelve phone calls, so here’s the long version while Harper is in the shower and Evan is pretending to do math.

This whole mobile makerspace thing at Lincoln has turned into my therapy, honestly. You remember how rough Fall 2023 was – three panic attacks in class between September and November, fun times. After that appointment with Dr. Nguyen at Family HealthCare Associates, when we talked about trying a low-dose anti-anxiety med and she referred me to short-term counseling, I started leaning hard on routines. Setting up the STEM carts, labeling bins in Room 214, letting kids tinker quietly – it all gave me something predictable when my heart was doing drum solos.

I’ve been joking that I should call the project “Staying After the Panic Attacks,” because those makerspace routines are what kept me from walking away from teaching last fall. Obviously I’m not putting that on any official paperwork, but it’s the truth.

At the same time, I’m trying to design the space with Harper in mind too. Her generalized anxiety and those awful group-work meltdowns at Valley Ridge in Spring 2023 are burned into my brain. I want my 6th–8th graders to have options: quiet, structured build roles for kids like her, and presentation roles for the hams.

How are things in Austin? Still surviving on tacos and cold brew? We’re thinking of driving down over spring break if Mom’s knee is behaving.

Love,
M

metadata.subject: Why I’m so wrapped up in this makerspace thing

artifacts.content:
[Thread with "Jess – 7th Sci"]

Melissa: You surviving benchmark season?

Jess: Barely. My grading pile is taller than my 3rd period.

Melissa: SAME. This is why I’m leaning so hard into the mobile makerspace this spring. If I can get kids working on longer projects, I can at least sneak in some grading while they’re soldering instead of babysitting worksheets all day.

Jess: 😂 I feel that.

Melissa: Honestly, part of the appeal is selfish. During testing season, if they’re in groups building recycling sorters or those accessibility gadgets we started after Evan’s broken-leg wheelchair adventure last October, they’re more independent and I can catch up on the mountain of paperwork.

Jess: You’re allowed to make your life easier.

Melissa: I keep telling myself that. The project idea is still kind of mushy in my head – I haven’t really mapped out day-by-day activities or lined everything up neatly with TEKS yet. I’m hoping once I see what the kids gravitate toward, I can backfill the “official” alignment.

Jess: That’s how half of my “innovations” start.

Melissa: Lol. Also, reminder: Thursday 3:30–4:30 is makerspace time with my probation diversion group. I have to log their 3 hours/week for Officer Hernandez, so if you see five 7th graders wandering the hall at 3:25, send them my way.

Jess: Will do. Coffee Friday?

Melissa: Yes please.

Hi Linda,

Quick update before I head to Harper’s art night.

I’ve been thinking a lot about why this makerspace feels so urgent. A bunch of my 7th graders this year are from the Willow Bend Drive apartments – same complex as us, just the rental side – and a lot of their families are crammed in with relatives right now. One of my ESL girls, Maria, has been doubled up with her aunt since her dad’s DUI arrest back in March 2023. They lost their car and fell behind on rent, and she has zero quiet space or computer time at home.

Those are the kids who light up when I roll the STEM cart in. They finally get to touch tech instead of just seeing it on a screen.

I’m also trying to be realistic about my own track record. I piloted a tiny version of this during homeroom last year – just a few cardboard engineering challenges – and it totally fizzled after a few weeks. I had trouble keeping everyone on task, and I didn’t do a great job of tracking what they were actually learning beyond “they looked engaged.” I’m determined to build in better structures and some simple reflection pieces this time so it’s not just chaos with hot glue.

Anyway, how are your 5th graders doing with their robotics club? If you have any simple templates for documenting learning, I’d love to steal them.

Miss our Friday debriefs,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Willow Bend kids & trying this makerspace thing again

artifacts.content:
Hi team,

I wanted to debrief today’s 7th period situation and float an idea.

As you know, four of my 7th period students were suspended for three days last March after the vaping mess in the 7th grade hallway and the follow-up investigation. We spent a lot of time in parent conferences and ISS trying to reset expectations, but I’m not convinced the kids see a positive path forward yet.

I’ve been toying with a project I’m calling (just in my head) “Second Chance STEM.” The idea is to use our Lincoln Middle School STEM lab and the mobile makerspace carts to re-engage that group, plus some of our other frequent flyers, in something they can own. Instead of them being “the vaping kids,” they’d be the ones designing, say, better hallway traffic flow or low-cost recycling systems.

This ties in with the after-school diversion group I’ve been hosting since January 2024. I’ve been signing weekly attendance sheets for Officer Hernandez from Denton County Juvenile Probation to document that those five students are doing their required 3 hours per week in the STEM lab instead of extra detention. I’m seeing glimmers of leadership from a couple of them when they’re soldering or troubleshooting code.

I know we have to be careful how we talk about all this with families, but I’d love your thoughts on building a structured “second chance” strand into our makerspace work.

Thanks for reading this long brain dump,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Idea: "Second Chance STEM" for our 7th period crew

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Anne Thompson <mthompson@lisd.net>
To: Daniel Scott Thompson <daniel.thompson@email.com>
Date: February 2, 2025
Subject: Rough grant narrative brain-dump

Hey Daniel,

I’m trying to pull all these threads into one makerspace grant, so I’m brain-dumping here.

Working titles are all way too personal, but they’re helping me clarify the story:

1) **Rebuilding After Evan’s October 2023 Concussion – Using Our Lincoln Middle School STEM Makerspace to Support Students with Post-TBI Learning Challenges.** Remember how Dr. Patel at Children’s Medical Center Dallas ordered 6 weeks of cognitive rest after the concussion? The post-concussion accommodations and all those neurology follow-up for TBI visits are what pushed me to use the Lincoln Middle School STEM Makerspace as a structured way for him (and other kids) to rebuild attention and memory.

2) **From Harper’s Dyslexia Diagnosis to District-Wide Innovation – A Lincoln Middle School Makerspace Approach to Supporting Struggling Readers.** Her formal dyslexia evaluation at Valley Ridge Elementary School in January 2024, plus the 3x per week pull-out sessions with Ms. Ramirez, their dyslexia specialist, totally changed how I think about reading-friendly design challenges.

3) **Closing the Gap After Daniel’s 3-Month Unemployment – Low-Cost STEM Makerspace Solutions for Underfunded Classrooms.** When your layoff notice hit and we went June–August 2023 with 3 months without Daniel’s salary, then had to absorb the $1,200 cut from my summer enrichment budget and cancel summer camps for the kids, it forced me into this obsession with low-cost builds we can do right in our garage at 7428 Willow Bend Drive.

Obviously I can’t use these exact titles on an official cover page, but they’re helping me shape the narrative.

More later,
M

From: Melissa Anne Thompson <mthompson@lisd.net>
To: Linda Marie Carson <lindacarson@email.com>
Date: February 9, 2025
Subject: Who this makerspace grant would actually serve

Hi Mom,

You asked who the grant is really for, so here’s the honest version (more detailed than I’ll ever put on paper).

The primary target group is my 7th‑grade science students from the 2024–2025 school year, including Evan Michael Thompson in my 3rd‑period class. After his anxiety diagnosis at Children’s Medical Center Dallas in May 2024, our school counselor, Ms. Ramirez, recommended placing him with a familiar teacher for added stability, so he joined my class in August 2024 at Lincoln Middle School. Officially I’ll just say “students needing social‑emotional support,” but you know the backstory.

The group also includes about 15 students in my 7th‑grade science sections who have IEPs for emotional/behavioral disorders. Two boys from my 5th‑period class were involved in a vaping and shoplifting incident at the Walmart on Round Grove Road in October 2023 and are still on behavior contracts monitored by Assistant Principal Mr. Delgado. In the grant, I’ll talk about “students with documented behavioral supports,” not “the Round Grove Walmart vaping/shoplifting kids,” even though that’s exactly who I’m thinking about.

Hands-on STEM in the mobile makerspace gives all of them—Evan, the EBD IEP kids, and my quieter Title I students—a safe space to channel their energy into something constructive.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Who this makerspace grant would actually serve

artifacts.content:
Personal planning doc – Makerspace Grant Narrative Draft
Last edited: March 3, 2025 – by Melissa Anne Thompson

Section: Project Description – DO NOT SUBMIT THIS VERSION

• Neighborhood safety unit: Have students analyze real data from the Lewisville Police Department’s October–December 2024 incident reports around Willow Bend Drive. Include the vandalism and attempted break-in at our house at 7428 Willow Bend Drive, Lewisville, TX 75067 on November 18, 2024, as a case study. Goal: use mobile makerspace carts to prototype low-cost sensor systems and community alert devices (simple microcontroller + LED/buzzer builds) tied to actual incident reports review.

• Ethics & digital citizenship: The project should explicitly respond to what happened with my 3rd-period 7th-grade science class in Fall 2024. Several of my 7th graders, including my son Evan Michael Thompson, got two-day in-school suspensions in October 2024 after they tried to 3D-print unauthorized key copies in the Lincoln Middle School STEM lab. Use this as an internal rationale only—disciplinary incident for 3D-printing unauthorized keys + in-school suspensions → need for structured challenges around ethical hacking, digital citizenship, and school safety.

REMINDER TO SELF:
– In official language, frame this as “students engaging in unsanctioned design experiments” and “discipline data indicating a need for ethics-focused STEM experiences.”
– Do NOT mention vandalism at our house, attempted break-in details, or the key-copy incident explicitly in the public-facing summary.
– Keep focus on skills: data analysis, collaborative design, and reflective discussion about consequences.
